text: Visible scarring is present at the five o'clock position from previous procedures. The recurrent fistula is observed with an internal opening, and a potential external opening may be visible at the top of the wound.

The surgeon exposes the internal opening of the fistula, revealing anorectal junction tissue above. A lacrimal probe is gently inserted into the internal opening, demonstrating the absence of muscle below the track running laterally into the buttock.
The surgeon uses a diaphane to cut down onto the probe, ensuring no muscle is divided as the area has been previously dissected.

The surgeon uses Emmet forceps to access an area of intersphincteric sepsis identified on MRI. The forceps are positioned at the entrance of the cavity, which measures approximately two to three millimeters in the intersphincteric space. The cavity is drained without involving any muscle tissue.

The video shows a complex anal fistula with a large external opening exhibiting significant overgranulation. The fistula track is visible, running from the front, down the right side of the anus, turning a corner towards the back, and leading to an internal opening. Pus is seen exuding from the opening, preventing epithelialization.

The surgeon identifies an overdrift at the corner of the fistula, noting an extra extension diverging from the primary tract. The primary tract is visibly wide, particularly noticeable about halfway along its length.

The surgeon uses a right-angle probe to navigate the internal opening of the fistula, initially pointing towards eight o'clock and then adjusting to nine o'clock as it enters the long track. The VAFT scope is introduced into the main track, equipped with a camera, light leads, and water ports, along with an instrument port for diathermy or other tools.

The surgeon attempts to navigate a probe through a complex fistula tract, encountering difficulty due to its shape. The probe reaches a cul-de-sac within the primary tract, prompting the surgeon to retract and attempt to turn the corner. At the corner, a seton is placed, aiming to shrink the tract for a sphincter-preserving procedure.
